# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Why don’t more people know the movie Summer School from 1987?

**Score:** 101
**Body:** It’s really a great movie and it holds up.  It’s an absurd comedy but it’s really warm hearted and uplifting in the end.  I love how the kids are not portrayed as morons or mean spirited.  They are actually really smart misunderstood kids.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Who doesn't love Chainsaw and Dave?!?
  - Only dirty, stinking commies don’t love Chainsaw and Dave! 
  - Chainsaw had a cameo on the 80s ski trip episode of always sunny a few years ago. I can’t imagine there was anyone who didn’t immediately get the reference.
2. My only guess is they didn’t have HBO in the 90s.
3. A young Mark Harmon and Courtney Thorne-Smith. If you had cable in late 80s,, then there's a pretty good chance you caught this movie as you nursed your hangover on a Sat or Sun afternoon (same with Cocktail, but that had Tom Cruise, which makes it more memorable IMO).
4. Underrated
5. Loved that. Rode my bike to the theater to see it. Back when Mark Harmon and Kirstie Alley could headline a movie. What a world.
6. This was one of my comfort movies in junior high 
7. Shoop, I PASSED!!! I was talking about this movie yesterday, after my roommate said she randomly screamed out loud in the car. Had to be done. It's time for a rewatch. 
8. This movie is so much fun

## 2. Devastating movies about addiction. Something like Leaving Las Vegas or Requiem For A Dream

**Score:** 45
**Body:** I’m not looking for anything like a romance or a comedy or anything light in tone. I want more heavy, visceral and disturbing movies. 

Leaving Las Vegas and requiem for a dream are 2 of my favorite movies and I just want more like them. I’m fascinated watching the spiral of addiction for personal reasons. 

I’m okay with reading subtitles and I will watch super old or new movies I’m not picky. Thank you 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Trainspotting 
  - It’s the reason I won’t do heroin. Poor Baby Dawn 
2. Basketball Diaries 
3. Oslo, August 31st (2011)

Beautiful Boy (2018)
4. Less Than Zero with Robert Downey Jr.  It still haunts me....
5. Spun
6. Drugstore Cowboy
7. Candy (2006)
8. Permanent Midnight 


The Salton Sea 
9. Christiane F. is probably the one i found the most fucked up. 

## 3. Looking for movies where the main character is inducted into a cult.

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Horror genre preferably

A sweet and happy main character, accidently or intentionally joins a group which turns out to be a creepy cult.

After that the movie explores all the sufferings the main character goes through, preferably with a good ending.

Any suggestions? Any language would be fine.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Midsommar (2019)
2. The Invitation (2015) – probably the closest match. The protagonist attends a gathering that slowly reveals itself to be cult-related. Great tension and a solid payoff.

  - God I love this movie. It’s a slow burn but when it gets there it goes so hard 
3. Martha Macy May Marlene. Ambiguous ending but incredible movie. 
4. Rosemary's Baby (1968), old but worth seeing
5. The Master
6. Sound of My Voice (2011) is excellent.

Let's Start a Cult (2024) was pretty bad.


The Testament of Ann Lee (2025) was something I did not get at all.
7. *Faults* (2014) is abt a young woman who’s already joined a cult, and her parents pay someone to deprogram her. 

Prefer not to say more than that except that its super worth a watch. And that it’s a fantastic showcase for Mary Elizabeth Winstead and Leland Orser.
8. Midsommar. 
9. On Becoming a God in Central Florida (2019).

## 4. Suggest me some good fish men horror

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Love fish men movies.

Ones I’ve seen/ heard of:  
Creature from the Black Lagoon 1-3  
The Shape of Water  
Humanoids from the Deep

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They're women, it's in Polish, and it's a musical horror film, but The Lure is awesome
  - Yk what I think they literally recommended this after I finished the Shape of Water last night so I might have to peep.
  - I’m an absolute sucker for good horror versions of fairytales and Disney stuff. 

Have you seen The Ugly Stepsister? it’s a Cinderella rework that focuses on one of the stepsisters whose trying to make herself attractive
    - Not yet, definitely on my list
2. The Mighty Boosh: Season 2, Episode 5

The Legend of Old Gregg
  - Kids these days have never drank Bailey’s out of a shoe
3. Dagon (2001) is pretty good. Just look past the questionable-at-times acting lol
4. Dagon (2001)

Pretty weird, pretty creepy, I didn't finish it cause it was a bit gruesome for me.
5. * **Underwater** - might fit. It's got a whole lot of fishy humanoid creatures because people dug too deeply.  Don't be turned of my the lead actress, she's really great in it. (Kristen Stewart)
6. One specific scene in House of 1000 Corpses lol 🧜🏻‍♂️ 

## 5. St. Vincent (2014)

**Score:** 12
**Body:** If you're looking for a great comfort movie that has a lot of heart but doesn't get too cheesy, I highly recommend St. Vincent.Bill Murray plays a grumpy, broke, and pretty text-book cynical old guy who unexpectedly ends up babysitting his new neighbor's 12-year-old kid. Jaeden Martell and Melissa McCarthy are both excellent in it too, and it's easily one of my favorite performances from McCarthy where she plays a grounded, more dramatic role.  It's incredibly funny but has some deeply moving, bittersweet moments that hit you out of nowhere. Definitely worth a watch if you haven't seen it yet!

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s very sweet.
2. Heartwarming: Song Sung Blue (peacock), One Chance, CODA

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Best response to a threat in a movie. I’ll start: Lucius Fox to Reese in The Dark Knight.

**Score:** 6017

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/8qmoziie2g6h1.jpeg?width=1002&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=b46021d8b34e0123cdd915209dcca1a34c3a229d
  - This whole exchange!!!
2. Shooter McGavin: "I eat pieces of shit like you for breakfast."

Happy Gilmore: "You eat pieces of shit for breakfast?"
3. None of you seem to understand ...
I'm not locked in here with you.
You're locked in here with me !
  - I also like the end:

Rorschach: You know we can't let you do that...

Ozymandias: ***Do that***, Rorschach!? I'm not a comic book villain... do you seriously think I'd explain my master stroke to you if there was even the slightest possibility you could affect the outcome? I triggered it 35 minutes ago
4. Vinnie Jones and his Desert Eagle in Snatch (2000) : "and the fact that you have "replica" written on the side of your gun..."
  - "And I, have Desert Eagle..Point 5 0. Written on the side of mine. Should precipitate the shrinking of your balls, along with your presence". Such a great line.
5. ![gif](giphy|PjRardeWVvHVK)
  - Ripley is the smartest horror film character ever. Everything she says makes perfect sense.
6. https://preview.redd.it/mimhx83zzf6h1.jpeg?width=460&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a57462f627d40052c962eed04a2d458e2a2a03ed

Christopher Walken in “Seven psychos” simply not doing what the bad guy wants and the bad guy just having no idea how to handle that

## 2. What's a bad shot in an otherwise amazing movie?

**Score:** 917
**Body:** Untouchables is incredible, from start to finish, and I never got tired to rewatch it.

However this shot where the bad guy falls to death is always ugly to me.

It's just a few seconds and it could have been avoided because the falling was filmed from other angles, as you can see in the scene.

Of course it doesn't ruin a classic.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dick Jones falling at the end of Robocop. Those weird, freakishly long arms.
  - https://i.redd.it/a0soku538h6h1.gif
2. Final scene from the original A Nightmare on Elm Street. Just a blow up doll going through a window.
3. Amazing movie? Debatable, but I love it.

However, this shot of Gerard Butler being thrown by an explosion in Olympus Has Fallen is goofy AF

https://preview.redd.it/2p07r25q5g6h1.png?width=2690&format=png&auto=webp&s=1afbe1d919b6cbc76c3cc74a35d890786e5357c2
4. https://preview.redd.it/6pgv66sn3g6h1.jpeg?width=1079&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=f62f8d86464240921415bc37d4a2c1b9a3d9fb70

  
LOTR: Fellowship of the Ring 
  - Looks like a dream sequence from a 1990s low-budget TV series 
  - I keep seeing this over and over on different posts but I don't see anything wrong with it.

It's beautiful, it shows Frodo waking up from a near death (undead) experience. He is saved/ healed by Lord Elrond and welcomed into his home. I stand by every frame of the trilogy, lol.

My example for a funny scene would be in Harry Potter and the Order of The Phoenix. It's the scene after Dumbledore v Voldemort fight, where Voldemort invades Harry's mind and does some funny movements in the  montage in that sand circle. I can't find a gif but I hope people know what I'm taking about, I'll add a pic.

https://preview.redd.it/ixsphmhweg6h1.jpeg?width=1080&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=dbed7ac634d55ed79276454aedb203124db3f50a
5. There was a movie titled "Pushing Tin" starring John Cusack and Billy Bob Thornton that I liked a lot.

Unfortunately, there's this one scene that's really fake-looking. Our two co-stars are standing on a runway and are blown away by wake turbulence. Kinda takes me out of the movie for a moment.

https://i.redd.it/101k7p509g6h1.gif


  - "Team rocket blasting off agaaaaaaiiiinnn!"
6. https://i.redd.it/xhzpu4w28g6h1.gif

## 3. Happy belated 65th birthday to Michael J. Fox!! What’s your favorite role from his filmography?

**Score:** 565

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Frighteners
2. Obviously Back to the Future is the most obvious answer but I love the Frighteners so much.
3. “You’re telling me you made a time machine… out of a Delorean?”

The pause. The expression. Burned into memory. 
  - "The way I see it, if you're gonna build a time machine into a car, why not do it with some style?"

![gif](giphy|Vqvr9BGv1vhDi)
4. Not film but he was great in his guest role on Curb Your Enthusiasm. 
5. Casualties of War
6. All of them
7. A toss -up between Mike Flaherty (Spin City) and Scott Wolf (Teen Wolf).
8. By the way this is Michael J. Fox. This was taken at the Actor Awards this year 
9. Spin City doesn’t get mentioned enough

## 4. What's your favorite moment or dialogue from the Scary Movie franchise?

**Score:** 324
**Body:** The above scene is from Scary Movie (2000).

There are several hilarious moments in the Scary Movie franchise, and one of my absolute favorites is Brenda's theater scene.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|RqWClkgTa9Fkc)
  - Crazy Mandela effect, one of the stronger ones I've experienced: he doesn't say "take my strong hand." He says ["take my little hand."](https://youtu.be/4OGZSr4_sBY?si=FaL3YLvqXUGeari_) Fucked me up when I discovered it a few weeks ago.
2. For me it was in Scary Movie 2:

• Caretaker (Hanson): "Oh, aren't those cool new skates. Now, you be careful with those. You don't want to fall and break something."
• Dwight: "Oh, that's funny. That's real funny. Um, let me give you a hand."
• Caretaker: "Well, that's awful kind of you. How about you give me a standing ovation? Why don't you lift me up?"
• Dwight: "Oh, okay. I see where you're going with this one. You look familiar to me. Were you in Stomp?"
• Caretaker: "Hey, you can kiss my grits."
• Dwight: "I think I'll be the bigger man now and walk away."Caretaker: "Walk... away."
3. Racking the shovel like a shotgun and Cindy speaking Japanese to the ghost boy
4. ![gif](giphy|3oz8xCDv3zIwP9A5R6)
5. “Hey, Tom! I need a ride home”
6. “Oh my god! We hit a boot!”

“Cindy! This is bones! Would you run from Calista Flockhart?!”

“I’m not crazy! AHHHHHHHHHH!”

“What’s his name? I might have already loved him.”

“Here! Take my little hand!” “No! Get it away from me!”

“Cindy! The TVs leaking! Cindy! This bitch is messing up my floor!”
7. Reveal of the alien and the reporter says "lets see that again" and the alien walks back in the frame going in opposite direction. 
8. Leslie Nielson pissing out of his finger 
9. Brenda’s fight against the Ring 😂😂😂

“Get up, you little ugly bitch!!!” lol 

## 5. This close-up shot of Betty Gabriel in Get Out is one of the most unnerving moments in modern horror cinema.

**Score:** 186
**Body:** The way tears ran down her face while she smiled was truly unsettling.

What's your favorite close-up shot from any genre where an actor brilliantly showcased layered emotions?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You could just have this shot by itself, with no one knowing the context, and it’s still incredibly disturbing and uncanny. It still elicits discomfort from the viewer. It’s perfection. 
  - smiling with the mouth crying with the eyes is a hard pull, A+
2. The grandpa "running" at night scene was wild and unnerving as well.

![gif](giphy|26gsv1iextbg5Gm5O)
  - Yeah, that's the part that got me. I hate it when things are coming AT you, and it freaked me out
3. I liked how even though her and the grandpa were apparently the leaders of the whole thing; because they were in black people's bodies they were still only servents. 
4. ![gif](giphy|ac7MA7r5IMYda)
5. ![gif](giphy|clsbxjJmxqjVRvg3Wm)


6. An excellently acted film all round
7. https://preview.redd.it/3ik5l3m4wh6h1.jpeg?width=686&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=7e94c95298265bf5a1f2247430d4ae3a33d0a176

Seok-Woo - Train to Busan
8. Crazy how he still hasn’t topped this movie. I don’t consider him a one hit wonder but this movie was so damn good I wouldn’t argue against it.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Nolan’s recent films all seem to be converging on the same visual style

**Score:** 197
**Body:** **Nolan’s recent films all seem to be converging on the same visual style**

Watching the trailer for The Odyssey, I had the same reaction I’ve had to Nolan’s last few films: regardless of genre, they increasingly look like variations of the same movie. Am I the only one who feels this way?

Dunkirk, Tenet, Oppenheimer and now The Odyssey all seem to have the same earthy, brown-grey, tactile large-format look. Muted colours, natural light, smoke, dirt, weathered materials. I think the best way to describe it is “physical realism.”

Obviously, the films don’t literally look identical. But Nolan’s visual style has become so dominant that the genre barely seems to change the overall aesthetic. A war film, a sci-fi spy film, a historical biopic and an ancient Greek epic all end up being filtered through the same desaturated, hyper-tangible visual language.

That’s what disappoints me most about The Odyssey. Greek mythology gives you so much room to create something strange, dreamlike, frightening, colourful or spiritually uncanny. But based on the trailer, it mostly looks like Dunkirk with bronze armour and wooden ships.

This is where Denis Villeneuve feels very different to me. He obviously has his own recurring style and sensibilities, but he seems more willing to let each story dictate its own visual world. Enemy looks nothing like Prisoners. Prisoners looks nothing like Sicario. Blade Runner 2049 looks nothing like Arrival, and neither looks like Dune.

You can still tell they’re Villeneuve films, but they don’t feel like the same visual template being applied to different genres.

With Nolan, it increasingly feels like the question is, “How do I make this story look like a Christopher Nolan film?”

With Villeneuve, it feels more like, “What should this particular story look and feel like?”

I still admire the craftsmanship in Nolan’s films, but visually, his recent work is starting to feel repetitive to me.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nolan's visual language has always been consistent, and it's always leaned heavily on the cinematographer he's working with. His work with Wally Pfister (everything up till The Dark Knight Rises) basically looked similar to one another: similar lighting (Pfister is a pretty unintrusive cinematographer who favors minimal but punchy sourcing, but doesn't call a ton of attention to himself), similar lens textures and palette, etc. He switched to Hoyte van Hoytema with Interstellar after Pfister made a go at directing, and his style is distinct enough (especially in his lighting) that there was a noticeable shift in Nolan's visual language at that time.

Villeneuve, by contrast, swaps cinematographers constantly: despite his career being much shorter, he's worked with André Turpin, Pierre Gill, Nicolas Bolduc, Roger Deakins , Bradford Young, Greig Fraser, and Linus Sandgren. What's more, he clearly gives them a fair amount of leeway given how much each of the films shot by those cinematographers resembles the other films shot by each of them with different directors.

Nolan's not really an outlier, and neither is Denis: there are plenty of directors who stick to one DP and plenty who don't, and additionally there are director/DP combos (Eggers/Blaschke is a good example) who self-consciously switch things up film-to-film, but the idea of a director/DOP honing a consistent visual language is by no means unusual. One could say the same for Spielberg/Kaminski, to name one: everything they've done, particularly in the DI era, tends to look very consistent accross multiple films.

Now, if the question was whether the Nolan/Hoytema house style was sufficiently *interesting*, I'm liable to be more critical, ha.
  - I disagree and think OPs point about Nolan is accurate. Compare Following, Memento, The Prestige, and Batman Begins to Dunkirk through The Odyssey. Nolan’s recent films are undeniably impressive on a technical level, but they often feel visually interchangeable. His early work had a unique look and atmosphere from film to film; his newer films, for all their craftsmanship, kinda come across as visually bland tbh
2. I agree with the majority of your point, but it’s interesting that you bring up Villeneuve as an example of the opposite. What you said about being disappointed by The Odyssey’s apparent lack of “something strange, dreamlike…or spiritually uncanny” is exactly my problem with his approach to the Dune movies. 

They’re extremely competently made, and add a much-needed dose of seriousness to science fiction in a landscape that has been marred by Marvel-like snark and winks to the audience. But there’s absolutely none of the bizarreness or psychedelia of Herbert’s original vision. It’s this very safe, straightforward, monochromatic rendering of his world that strikes me as a tad boring and unimaginative.

The dream/prophecy sequences, which should’ve been an opportunity to untether from the stoic realism of the rest of the films, end up looking like perfume commercials to me. There’s a slick minimalism in his Dune films that verges on brutalism, to the point that there’s no real stylistic distinction between the Harkonnen and Atreides armies. 

It’s telling that the most visually distinctive moment in both films is the switch to black-and-white for Feyd’s fight in the gladiator arena. I get that Villenueve is being intentional in stripping down his sci-fi to stand apart from the goofiness and poppy colors of something like Guardians of the Galaxy, but when you see Lynch’s Dune or the concept art for Jodorowsky’s planned adaptation, it just strikes me as dull and overly sterile by comparison. 

That’s why, as flawed as Lynch’s Dune is, I still find it to be the more interesting and imaginative adaptation. The choices he made regarding makeup, costumes, production design, special effects, etc. feel distinctive in a way that Villenueve’s films just aren’t. I’d be curious to know why The Odyssey’s visual palette strikes you as being boring or samey but Dune’s doesn’t. 
  - Also, incredibly, the Dune films for some reason don't even feel "hot" (kind of a vital thing to capture). They feel temperature-less. Yes, beautiful, dusty, everyone in their suits...but nobody is actually hot.
    - It drives me nuts to see people praise the visuals of these movies. Like yeah, the ornithopter is cool, the sand is rendered well, the worm is, fine. But man, read one chapter anywhere in Dune and there is so much more detail and psychedelia packed into it that I don't know how anyone is satisfied with his Dune.
  - Villeneuve seems like a control freak. Maybe Nolan is too. They have all these refined movie-making tools and massive budgets and want to make everything *perfect*... and a little safe and boring (goes for other creative choices as well, imho). Huge online fan bases commenting on every single frame don't help either.

That's my gut feeling anyway, lol.
3. I see what you mean. They all feel like they were made by someone who really enjoyed the cinematography of Interstellar. I think it works fine, as they share a specific "cinematic" look which lends to the epic feeling of the grand narratives you see play out across the films you've mentioned. But it does feel a little uninspired. 

Interstellar was a film about human extinction, perseverance, and some of the realest and most raw feelings of the human condition. It really earns that sense of scale. The others sort of chase it in a way. 
4. He's always seemed like a director who's focused more on the technical aspects of film. That's not a bad thing. I'd put James Cameron, George Lucas, and Robert Zemeckis in that category too.  Decent storytellers who are looking for different tools to incorporate into their process. But it does feel more and more like Nolan has rested on his laurels in favor of spectacle. Not sure what that is, other than maybe he found a formula that works for him and applies it to everything he's done for the last 10 years. 

I would love to see him take more chances (not just by working with bigger budgets) because he's still one of a handful of directors that can pull an audience in on name recognition alone. 
  - Nolan is not a very technical filmmaker or a perfectionist, at least compared to directors like Cameron or Fincher. He doesn’t storyboard outside of complex sequences. He never waits for the weather. He’s not interested in cutting-edge effects. He doesn’t even like using a video village or lav mics. 

His interest seems to be in the raw material quality of the medium — literally using the highest resolution film possible. Well-known archetypes and narrative structures are used as shorthand and rearranged in his stories. His style is the cinematic equivalent of brutalist architecture.

It’s hard to pin down how exactly all this adds up, but the vibe when you watch his films is a sense of massive but relatable scale. To me, his movies feel very real and tactile in a way that few others do.

I think most people, including his fans, don’t really appreciate this aspect of his style and how hard it is to really nail.
  - Its interesting problem, when directors (or artists, novelists , bands etc)  get successful the natural tendency is to repeat what made them famous -sometimes because thats all the money guys will allow them..

Personally I just want great stories and entertainment - ie the storytelling above anything else. Next I want a diversity of stories..ie epics, small stories, stories about fantasy places and fictional worlds sure, ie the more the merrier IF they are done well.. like I love the success of Backrooms for example. unfortunately Hollywood rewards monetary success with formula and regurgitation (prepare for Backrooms and Obsession sequels and clones) - I don't think any of the directors you mentioned want formula but sometimes its all the budget is there for..

Speaking of directors on your list, Lucas had a lot of talent but seemed to get bogged down in a galaxy far far away - but he is a genius for the technical - ILM, Pixar etc. Most of modern blockbuster moviemaking can be traced to things he developed along

Cameron had some diversity in his spectacle movies.. from superb action, to action comedy to action scifi but they alway had a bombastic epicness to them. Titanic is not a movie I would have expected from him (unless I know his love for the ocean)  He's also spent too much time on one series (Avatar) but he knows how to put a story together.

Zemekis is interesting he never repeats himself. He's super talented but has a had a lot of failures, becuase of that but I kind of love them, eg welcome to marwen, beowolf, here -all so different. He got a little to enamored with cgi cartoons at one point too. But his successes like Back to Future, Forrest Gump, Castaway etc are all move standards. ie examples of the best movies ever made.

Spielburg. -who you did not mention can also be super technical but has this genius for storytelling that works across so many varieties of films.. I mean he made Schindler's list and Jurassic park in the same year

Vellenuve is interesting .. in many ways like Speilburg in that he tackles all sorts of stories. but his movies don't all feel the same..

Now Nolan -who def belongs on this list.. does tend to lean into technical epic moviemaking, he's not a poetic or cinema driven auteur - but a master of planning and organization and execution -ie I expect the oddessy to have a lot of epic set pieces perfectly planned.. but maybe some of the color, humanity and diverse spectacle will be lost.. not sure. the trailers seem a bit bland.. however I dont really think Nolan has ever really made a bad movie. Even Tenant was fasicnating and watchable -if a bit hard to decipher and opaque emotionally.

## 2. What are some genre or even exploitation films with technical/virtuoso filmmaking?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** As a lover of both arthouse and grindhouse, I've always been fascinated with the idea of directors applying high brow techniques and stylistic approach to what would be essentially considered low brow films by many critics.

A good example of this is Dario Argento. Suspiria and Inferno are impressive for their use of colored lighting, but his most technically astounding to me is Opera. Despite being a violent giallo/slasher, I genuinely think that film has some of the best camerawork I've ever seen in cinema. To a lesser extent all Argento until 1987 (+ Sleepless) could apply, as well as other Italian giallo/horror directors like Sergio Martino in the early 70s, or Michele Soavi in the late 80s.

Another example, this time from the US, would be Brian De Palma. Dressed To Kill, Blow Out, Body Double, Raising Cain... all schlocky (in a good way) Hitchcock-inspired thrillers (with a prominent giallo influence, coincidentally) with absolutely impressive mise en scene. Weirdly enough, he's most popular for the crime epic Scarface which I find slightly less visually inventive and stylized.

As a third example, I need to bring up Gaspar Noé, the enfant terrible of European cinema (I know people say the same thing about Lars Von Trier, Michael Haneke and even Ruben Ostlund but I think Noé is the most genuinely provocative of the bunch). Impressively fluid handheld camerawork, long takes, great blocking in the rare case the camera is still, interesting use of aggressive strobe lighting, great sound design and it's all in service of... a Memento-structured rape revenge? A porn film about a failed love triangle? An exploitation film about a bad drug trip? Despite the premises of his movies, Noé demonstrates tremendous technical effort and talent.

What are some other examples of this? Of course these are all quite well known, but it's a phenomenon you can find in underground filmmaking too. I'm not an expert on the genre but some classic Japanese pinku films have some impressive use of lighting and cinematography, and they're essentialy sexploitation movies. It's always interesting when this happens, in my opinion.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hausu (1977) has particularly lovely camera work and practical camera effects. 

You could say the same for Prisoner 701 Scorpion (1972) its not a great movie, but stylistically it shines in places with camera work and colours

Street trash (1987) has some really special stuff too, you can see the accomplished film maker Jim Muro was to become before he went on to Hollywood mega movies.

Intruder (1989) really stands out, it uses some unique and unusual camera tricks and angles that I've never seen repeated. 
2. Abel Ferrara has such a weird blend of experimental artsy stuff and grimy genre work. Some of his films lean more in one direction or the other, of course. The King of New York is a pretty straightforward gangster film that any fan of the genre would be able to appreciate, and some of his earlier exploitation films like The Driller Killer and Ms .45 aren’t exactly known for their experimental style. New Rose Hotel, on the other hand, is a decidedly Weird experimental film. 

But some of his films combine the vibes in a totally unique way that makes you feel like you’re watching a movie from an alternate dimension where traditional boundaries between exploitation films and art films are completely absent. The Addiction, for example, is a grimy vampire movie that has one of the guys from the gangsta rap group Onyx as an actor, and songs by Onyx and Cypress Hill on the soundtrack. Sounds like a fun genre piece full of NY and hip-hop culture like King of New York, right? But it’s filmed in black and white, and the dialogue is so densely packed with references to all kinds of philosophers and authors that I struggled to follow what the protagonist was talking about half the time. It uses vampirism as a metaphor for everything from sexual assault to drug addiction to the exploitation of the Global South by privileged Westerners. It is one of the most ambitious and thoughtful vampire movies I’ve ever seen, and I’ve seen a LOT of them. And the grittiness and artsiness feel like they should conflict in some way, but they mesh together effortlessly. Ain’t nobody makin shit like that except Abel Ferrara. 
3. To me Faster Pussycat!, Kill! Kill! (1965) is a cinema masterpiece. The camera angles, the richness of the photography, the cutting, and then the building of the archetypes really all fits together in very powerful ways. Yes exploitation, but really put together to have a powerful aesthetic effect. It impresses. 
4. the *female prisoner scorpion* movies (particularly 1&3) and a whole lot of seijun suzuki's output could definitely qualify. also the *transgressor* (AKA *school of the holy beast*) and masumura's *blind beast.* 

i hesitate to namedrop melville despite how much i dig his films and how much of his filmography is literally genre films as they're far, far classier than anything that comes to mind when i think "lowbrow/genre films that go beast mode with the camera". 

costa-gavras debut feature -- the wildly underrated and super stylish pulp flick *the sleeping car murders* \-- might be a better borderline pick in the realm of euro genre films, somewhere alongside the 1960s run of black and white edgar wallace krimis that occasionally get quite creative with their cinematography. 

mario bava's filmography is another good place to look and might be pretty unique as he was cinematographer for many/most of his directorial efforts too
5. John Woo’s Gun Fu (\*Heroic Bloodshed\*)  cinema of the 80’s/90’s.

At its core they should be trashy B action movie a cheap copy of the noir thrillers of old.

But Woo brings this operatic flair to them that makes it way more sophisticated than they should be, it should be silly but I really buy into the melodrama in films like The Killer and Bullet in the Head. 

And the action shootouts are still my favourites in cinema, it’s like a ballet of death just chaos. 
6. Most of Michael Mann's work but especially Miami Vice 2006. Slick straightforward action piece about hot muscle guys who are so good at guns and planes and boats etc that just dissolves into an abstract tragedy. The plot keeps happening sort of but matters less and less you just soak in this haunted trapped apocalyptic feeling. Not to mention one of the best looking most sensual digital movies ever filmed. The spray of saltwater the bullets sparking as they rip thru cars the streak of blood from a freeway suicide... Dion Beebe is a god it's crazy how he went from this and Collateral (also perfect but a *little* bit more self consciously arty even if its mainstream legacy is "whoah that was badass when tom cruise shot those guys really fast") to lameass disney remakes.


Also someone else mentioned John Woo so I'll shout out Jonnie To for the Hong Kong connect. Throw Down is an all timer. The way the tension breaks when Sze-to and Tony finally fight and it goes into that beautiful montage... The last scene in the field....
7. I love a lot of the movies you mentioned, and something that jumps off the page for me to recommend would be Vengeance Is Mine (1979). It's a Japanese serial killer movie that I still think about some of the trapeze work they must have done to do these long, sometimes very fast shots through the different narrow japanese houses and hallways. Very fun
8. Evil Dead of trilogy has always been so impressive for me by how much charisma/identity has only by the camera movements/framing.

Sam Raimi has a really precise, chaotic yet never random vision of how his movies work and look that is just so inspiring and amazing.

I also love how, specially the first one, never looks cheap. It uses the few resources they have to feel actually gritty and raw in a certain way 

I love how the night is filmed in an almost pitch dark environment yet you can actually see through it with how the heavy contrast was used. 

## 3. Disclosure Day disappointment

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Was very excited for this movie from the trailer.

I’m a huge Spielberg fan but this felt closer to his film AI in quality and that movie atleast had more to it. In general bar some moments it didn’t even feel like one of his movies. 

The pacing and story felt quite rushed. It genuinely felt more like a series of moments than a true narrative. The closest comparison I can make is that first suicide squad film where things are just sliced in. 

Nothing felt very developed. You’re somewhat thrown in to the middle of an existing plot where everything is incredibly condescend.

The great reviews I’m honestly astonished by. I can kind of see why some may enjoy the film but over all I just couldn’t personally get into this movie. 

The acting was largely good. I like a lot of the camera work. But in its entirety it was a mediocre film in my own opinion. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Really? Maybe it's cause I've always been into this 70s/90s style co-mingling of ESPer-ish alien type sci-fi and crises of faith that go along with it, but I found the character choices and story line here really well done amd really engaging. Not sure if Emily Blunt was the right performer for this (though by the end she did pull me in quite a lot). 


My personal big sticking point was the shitty CGI animals. I dunno why every goddamn director under the sun right now apparently has a big hard on for ugly CGI deer especially lol

I thought the trailers looked terrible but the movie itself was really good.
  - I agree here, I enjoy the movie a lot but the biggest criticism I have is the cgi.  I also do think it felt a hair long, but nothing enough to detract.    
I did enjoy Blunt though!
  - I was forgiving of the animals CGI when it revealed what they were. BUT yes it was woeful. Even the car scene with the trailer.

In a fan of the genres you listed. But this felt more like a surface skim of these for my own personal taste. If it had a slower reveal or build up it may have worked for me. 
2. I had a similar reaction. where the trailer promised something more  emotionally engaging, but the final product felt surprisingly uneven. There were flashes of Spielberg's trademark strengths, especially in the visual storytelling and a few genuinely affecting moments, yet on the other hand   they never fully came together into a satisfying whole. The pacing was one of the biggest issues for me;)  , important thinga seemed rushed while other scenes were just there, boring without adding much depth. I also struggled to connect with the characters because the film felt like assumed investment before properly earning it. I can understand why critics appreciated its ambition and themes, but I was left feeling that the action didn't match the potential. Not terrible, just disappointingly average.
  - Your last sentence perfectly describes this movie.  Disappointingly average. 

As a Gen Xer who grew up watching all of the Spielberg gems, it's hard to see such a lazy effort. 
3. I think my biggest problem with the film was that the action scenes (what few there are) could have been cut and pasted into any other film. There's very little unique about them, very little that ties into the aliens or sci-fi aspect. The invisible fire engine scene the only one (and honestly was kind of dumb). Otherwise it's generic car chase, generic swat team raid, generic oncoming train/shootout scene. 
4. Idk I guess people are excited because it's Spielberg but I thought the trailers for this looked pretty generic. Especially the extended trailer which played in my theatre previews, seemed like it spoiled the entire thing and kind of knocked any of the wind that it had in its sails for me personally. 


5. Spielberg has been off his game for a decade or more now. This was always going to be the confused musings of a retirement-age director thinking about “the universe and shit”. It’s in part why I think the late career masterpieces of Scorsese should be more studied and revered.
  - The Fabelmans is excellent. 
6. I thought the trailers looked awful. I hate David Koepp. I have no expectations for this movie. BUT, these reviews are befuddling me. How is this getting such good reviews? Is it just Spielberg? Is it actually somehow a good movie? Who knows, but I’m probably not watching it in theaters to find out.

## 4. Did anybody else dislike Obsession(but not in a hater way)?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I want to emphasize that I do not want to start a hate train on this movie. I actually thought it was really good. I'm not a cinephile but I was immersed in the movie. But there was something about this movie that struck a specific chord with me that I couldn't deal with. I usually like horror. I wouldn't say that I'm a big horror fan, but I go and see horror movies with good reviews and usually like them.

For this movie, I had to leave before it ended. I've never done that, at least at a theater. I read a lot of the reviews for this movie, and a lot of people mentioned how they got chills, how unsettling it was, the uncomfortable feelings they got from it. I also got all of those, but usually when I watch horror movies, there is also this undertone of excitement. This time I kind of just felt a sinking feeling the whole time. And this isn't meant to knock on the people who enjoyed it because, like I mentioned, it was a well executed movie(unless you enjoyed it for weird reasons). Like I said, I'm not looking to hate on the movie, it was probably just not for me! I'm just looking for people to relate to and discuss with. I'm having a hard time articulating how I feel about this movie. I also feel like this can be a touchy subject which is why I'm over explaining.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think for some people a horror move giving them a “sinking feeling” is that movie succeeding at being a horror movie. For you, it was too much. I am just happy this movie is well written enough that it is engaging people that are not normally predisposed to horror.
  - Yeah, exactly. Getting that feeling is rare these days for me and Obsession provided it in certain scenes, so I enjoyed it a lot. Although I do have to say that I understand getting overwhelmed by it. I had to turn off The Coffee Table after 30 minutes, because it just kept building dread inside me.
  - I didn't know anything about the film before I saw it except that it was a about a "crazy girlfriend" is the broadest of terms.

Movie left a pit in my stomache after seeing it, from the sheer misery of what happens to the deeply unsettling movement of the girlfriend (IYKYK).

It was extremely effective and now I don't plan on watching it again. Haven't had a horror do that since I was a boy.

Five stars.
2. For me, I enjoyed that it was a horror movie that wasn’t writing in little quips to break the tension, or trying to be palatable.

It was disturbing at times, conflicting at others, with Characters who were very “neutral” in how the audience could either demonize them or empathize with them.

I find most of the criticisms to be people criticizing the movie for things that the movie isn’t.

I wouldn’t say you have a criticism, more that it’s not your type of movie. Which is fine.
3. I think it's best to avoid being too online with respect to new films. I saw it and enjoyed it and have not encountered this apparently toxic fan base at all, but I could see how if I had, it could color the experience to some extent. But ultimately you'll be best off engaging with the film directly, not mediated through some broader internet discourse. Go in relatively blind and form your opinions before reading reviews.

As for the critiques you raised, I didn't find it to be quite as you described it. It's certainly a frightening movie but there's also a lot of humor in there, akin to Zach Cregger's films. It's going to depend on the audience but there was definitely laughter in the theater when I went. A film that I saw where I did have the experience you were describing was Sirat, which was so bleak and unforgiving that I felt like it was bordering on cruel to the viewer (though I still stayed through the end and ultimately felt it was a very good, if flawed, film).
  - OMG, Sirat was fucking ROUGH. I was exhausted after watching that one. Had to have been one of the most intense films I've seen in a while. Terrifying.
4. >**This time I kind of just felt a sinking feeling the whole time.** And this isn't meant to knock on the people who enjoyed it because, like I mentioned, it was a well executed movie(unless you enjoyed it for weird reasons). 

I don't think you're over explaining at all. You're explaining exactly what the movie is going for, and you acknowledge it was well-executed but didn't click with you. 

Whenever I have trouble articulating how I feel about a film, I start running counterfactuals. What would have had to have changed to make me enjoy it more? A few questions that might help you (and us understand you)

1. What did you like most about it?
2. What did you actively dislike (besides the sinking feeling)?
3. What point in the film did you leave at?
4. Describe a time in the film where you thought something was going to happen, and it didn't in a way that disappointed you
5. I'm really confused by the essays about the characters' personalities and stuff. No shade at all, but I don't think they were going very deep with any of that. Because I feel like it's beside the point of the story the movie is trying to tell. Not everything has to have a deeper meaning, a lot of movies are just there to entertain and that's perfectly fine. Didn't HATE it, but didn't really love it either
6. That "sinking feeling" is absolutely right. But that isn't a negative for me. It's a tragedy. 

From the moment the guy on the phone said "you could kill yourself", Bear was going to die. It was all about seeing how he would get there. 
7. I liked it and sat through the whole thing but I also seriously considered leaving a couple of times. 

You’re right it’s not fun horror. Even The Shining or Exorcist or Paranormal Activity can be fun. But Obsession and also Bring Her Back (Talk to Me to an extent) were more disturbing. 

I wouldn’t put these three above the first three I mentioned. I prefer the former in terms of craft and storytelling. Bring Her Back is maybe the most flawed out of the latter three.

I think with Obsession part of it is that the protagonist is the real monster and we’re with him the whole time while the movie monster is actually a woman trapped in her own body. It makes it so uncomfortable.
